tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Tell all the people around the world, Jack, and also in Houston, MCC, what a great job they've done, and we'll see them back there shortly.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Thank you and their congratulations to you and your crew.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We'll wait until we get onboard the carrier for that.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Mission audio not available from this point until 187:11:08.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Do you want me to maintain High Gain for you until after the update?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We prefer the High Gain for the update.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I have some attitudes for P52.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We're still doing some stowing here.
Go ahead and give me a rough one, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The P52 - We have two attitudes.
Probably the preferred is the one I'll read first and if you want another one, I'll give it to you.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Roll, pitch, and yaw are all balls, and your stars are Menkent, number 30, Atria, number 34, and Nunki, number 37.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Roll, pitch, and yaw all balls, stars 30, 34, and 37.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 187 hours, 11 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Apollo 10 now 36,136 nautical miles [66.924 km] out from Earth.
Velocity continuing to build up; now 10,709 feet per second [3,264 m/s].
Estimated velocity at Entry Interface, or 400,000 feet [121,920 metres] above the surface; 36,314 feet per second [11,070 m/s].
Ignition time for midcourse correction number 7, 1 hour and 37 minutes away.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I'll stay in this attitude until you give us the update, and then we'll go down to zero, zero, zero.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We've got a state vector and entry REFSMMAT and a midcourse 7 target load, and if you will go to Accept.
In addition, we're not reading anything on the VHF yet, and we'd like you to ensure that the VHF is cranked up.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: MCC-H are updating the CMC reference matrix (REFSMMAT) for the last time, in readiness for the entry.]
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We are in P00 and Accept.
I've got the VHF warming up and we'll give you a call in just a minute.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The VHF/AM system will be needed for voice communication between the spacecraft and the recovery forces when they come out of communications blackout.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo 10 on VHF A Simplex.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I hear you loud and clear, 10, on VHF A.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: This is 10 reading you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I think I'm getting you on S-band.
We'll check with the VHF people.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Carnarvon reads you loud and clear on VHF.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I'm reading you about three-by-three on VHF.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I'm reading you here on S-band, and we'll have to check with the sites to see how the VHF is coming in.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: How do you read on VHF?
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Apparently you weren't reading us on VHF.
We'll give you another VHF check in about a half an hour.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I'll go ahead and pitch down to 000 when you have everything the [garble] as far as the uplink is complete and verified.
I guess it is verified now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The uplink is complete, Tom, and you're clear to the new attitude.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: This attitude of 000 will be a good one to torque to the new REFSMMAT?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We'd like you to go to wide beamwidth and High Gain Antenna and I have a maneuver PAD and an entry PAD for you.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We'll be with you in one second; I'm going to wide beamwidth right now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: You're in wide, Houston, and we'll give you a call when we're ready to copy.
We're still doing a few little chores here.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Reformatted version of the entry checklist
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're ready to copy your P30 and your entry update.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Noun 33 is 188:49:56.75; plus three balls 16, minus all balls, minus four balls 1; three balls, 129, three balls; apogee is NA, perigee is plus 0021.2; three balls 16, two balls 7, three balls 16; 40, 295.9, 38.3; 033, Uniform 159, Lima 12.
The rest is NA; your set stars are Deneb, 43; Vega, 36; 067, 174, 343.
Your ullage is a two-quad burn, use Bravo and Delta.
Read back maneuver PAD; let me know when you're ready with the entry PAD.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: MCC 7, RCS G&N: 25232; 48's NA; 188:49:56.2 - .75, plus three balls 16, minus all balls, minus four balls 1.
Apogee is NA; perigee is plus 0021.2, three balls 16, two balls 7, three balls 16; 40, 295.9, 38.3; 033, Uniform 159, Lima 12; Deneb, 43 and Vega, 36; 067, 174, 343.
Using two quads, Bravo and Delta.
And I'd like to read Noun 33 back to you again.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The PAD is interpreted as follows:Purpose:This PAD is for MCC-7, the only midcourse correction opportunity used during the trans-Earth coast.Systems:The burn will be made using the SM RCS (Reaction Control System) under the control of the Guidance and Navigation system.CSM Weight (Noun 47):25,232 pounds (11,445 kg).Pitch and yaw trim (Noun 48):As this is not an SPS manoeuvre, the pitch and yaw trim angles for this engine are not required.Time of ignition, TIG(Noun 33):188 hours, 49 minutes, 56.75 seconds.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s]:X, +1.6 (0.49); Y, -0.0 (0.0); Z, -0.1 (0.03).
The change in velocity is resolved into three components expressed relative to the local vertical frame of reference.Spacecraft attitude:Roll, 000°; Pitch, 129°; Yaw, 000°.
The desired spacecraft attitude is measured relative to the alignment of the guidance platform.HA, expected apogee of resulting orbit (Noun 44):Not applicable.
The trajectory being modified has no sensible apogee, at least in terms of the computer.HP, expected perigee of resulting orbit (Noun 44):21.2 nautical miles [39.6 km).
This will be their altitude at closest approach according to the trajectory resulting from this burn.
As this altitude is within the Earth's atmosphere, re-entry will take place.Delta-VT:1.6 fps (0.49 m/s].
This is the total change in velocity the spacecraft would experience.
(It is a vector sum of the three components given above.)Burn duration or burn time:7 seconds.Delta-VC:1.6 fps (0.49 m/s].
This value is entered into the Delta-V display of the EMS (Entry Monitor System) panel.
They will watch this figure descend to zero as the RCS fires, giving them a confirmation of the burn's progress.Sextant star:Star 40 (Altair) visible in sextant when shaft and trunnion angles are.
This is part of an attitude check.Boresight star:Star 033 for manoeuvre attitude check using the COAS.
The COAS viewing angles for the boresight star are 15.9° up, and 1.2° left.GDC Align stars:Stars to be used for GDC Align purposes are Deneb (43) and Vega (36).
These would be used in the event that the IMU could not be used for aligning the gyro assemblies.
The spacecraft would be manoeuvred so that the two stars could be viewed in the scanning telescope in a certain configuration.

On achieving this, the spacecraft would be in a known attitude, the same one given in the PAD, allowing the GDC to be manually aligned.GDC Align angles:Roll, 67°; pitch, 174°; yaw, 343°.As this is such a small Delta-V (velocity) correction, MCC-H are suggesting only using two of the four SM RCS quadrants to perform this manoeuvre; quads Bravo & Delta on opposite sides of the SM.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Your horizon is dark at Entry Interface.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The lift vector is Up; using a nonexit pattern and the horizon is dark at Entry Interface.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I have a late correction at the bottom.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We'd like you to read the range to go back, please.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The crew have a form available to fill out with the Entry PAD.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The PAD is interpreted as follows:Purpose:Entry.Landing target:The landing target is in the Mid-Pacific.IMU gimbal angles required for trim at 0.05g:Roll, 0°; pitch, 153°; yaw, 1°.Time of the horizon check:191 hours, 31 minutes, 54 seconds GET.Spacecraft pitch at horizon check:268°.

This is 17 minutes before time of entry.Splashdown point (Noun 61):15.07° north latitude, 164.67° west longitude.Maximum number of gs during entry:6.8g.Velocity at Entry Interface (400,000 feet altitude, Noun 60):36,315 feet/second (11,070 m/s).Entry flight path angle at Entry Interface:6.52°.Range to go (RTGO) to splashdown point from 0.05g event:1,204.1 nautical miles.
This figure is intended for the EMS Range To Go counter.Predicted inertial velocity (VI0) at 0.05g event:36,395 feet per second.
This is another entry for the EMS.

It is entered into the unit's Delta-V counter and will be decremented based on signals from its own accelerometer.Time of Entry Interface:191 hours, 48 minutes, 54 seconds GET.Time from Entry Interface (400k feet (121,920 metres) to 0.05g event:0:28 (seconds).As this is a non exit entry, Noun 69 is not applicable.Planned drag level (D0) (deceleration) during the constant g phase:4.00g.Time from Entry Interface until their velocity slows sufficiently to allow a circular orbit around the Earth:2:08.
The practical implication of this is that this is the "capture point" where the CM cannot exit on an orbit around Earth.

It is bound to reach the surface on this pass.Time from Entry Interface that the communications blackout begins:0:18.Time from Entry Interface that the communications blackout ends:3:29.Time from Entry Interface that the drogue parachutes will deploy:8:17.Sextant star:40 (Altair)Sextant shaft angle at Entry Interface minus 2 minutes:262.1°.Sextant trunnion angle at Entry Interface minus 2 minutes:34.7°.Boresight star:Star 033 for manoeuvre attitude check using the COAS.

The COAS viewing angles for the boresight star are 8.0° down, and 2.2° left.Lift vector at Entry Interface:Up.Additional comments are that they should use a non-exit EMS scroll pattern, and that the horizon will be dark at the time of Entry Interface as they are travelling towards the sunrise terminator.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: To translate some of the exchange of numbers between the spacecraft and the ground; they were passed up a maneuver PAD for midcourse correction burn number 7; will be with the RCS thrusters.
At 188 hours, 49 minutes, 56.75 seconds, 1.6 foot per second [0.49 m/s] velocity change, burn time of 7 seconds.
The additional - the other PAD for the entry numbers to the Mid Pacific prime recovery line, nominally along 165 west longitude, showing target point of 15.07 south latitude, 164.67 west longitude.
Maximum gs expected, 6.8; velocity at 400,000 feet [121,920 metres] or Entry Interface, 36,315 feet per second (11,070 m/s]; flight path angle at 400,000 feet [121,920 metres], 6.52 degrees.
The range to go at the time the Entry Monitor System is activated, 1,204.1 nautical miles [2,230.0 km].
Velocity at the time the Entry Monitor System is activated and the crew begins reading the onboard displays of range to go, steering commands and so on, 36,395 feet per second (11,093 m/s].
Begin blackout, 18 seconds after Entry Interface.
End black out; 3 minutes, 29 seconds after Entry Interface.
Drogue deploy; 8 minutes, 17 seconds after Entry Interface.
They were also informed that the horizon would be dark at Entry Interface.
Now 187 hours, 52 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Showing 57 minutes, 40 seconds, mark, to ignition of midcourse correction burn number 7; 3 hours, 56 minutes, 28 seconds to Entry Interface.
Distance now 31,956 nautical miles [59,182 km] out from Earth; speed continuing to increase to now reading 11,336 feet per second [3,455 m/s].
Continuing to monitor air to ground circuit for continuing conversation of Apollo 10 crew, with spacecraft communicator Jack Lousma here in Mission Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We know the set - the DAP is now set up for four jets, and we think we ought to have two jets.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: A two-jet ullage was specified in the MCC-7 PAD.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You want - Since it's only a couple of feet per second here and everything, we've got plenty of fuel, but well go ahead and use two jets.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We're going into the cold soak in our checklist, there.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: What's your feeling about putting fuel cell 1 on for the burn?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We don't need fuel cell 1 for the burn and don't intend to use it for the burn.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're starting through our P30 series now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The prethrusting program, Program 30 (P30 - External Delta-V) is run to enter the basic parameters such as the time of ignition and change in velocity, which was provided in the MCC-7 PAD.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We suspect that your suit circuit heat exchanger may be in the Bypass position and - well, for comfort, it ought to be on now, and go to Bypass at EI minus 50.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: When it's convenient, we'd like to get a GET time hack with you.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Go ahead and give us a time and we'll set our clocks.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: That's computer time and GET time, and our mission time is right on.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We're following right along with you.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We're going to go ahead and call up P41.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Program 41 (P41 - RCS Thrusting) is now selected.
P41 and uses the data computed by P30 to calculate the steering commands, manoeuvre the spacecraft to the burn attitude and control the RCS burn.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, 188 hours, 39 minutes Ground Elapsed Time, coming up on 10 mark to the burn, Mark, 10 minutes to ignition of midcourse correction burn number 7, air-to-ground still live.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Would you give us a hack at 5 minutes prior to the maneuver just so we can recheck our event timer?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Understand you want a time hack just prior to the burn.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: This event timer occasionally has been jumping numbers.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We'll give you a hack at 5 minutes.
That'll be about a minute and a half yet.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're proceeding here for the final trim.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Come all the way back to the Moon and do this kind of midcourse.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: That's pretty good shooting, isn't it?
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Man, I mean to tell you.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: and company looks like they have a pretty good rifle scope there to shoot us back in this target.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Stafford is referring to the then Director of Flight Operations, Christopher C.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I guess all that mathematics really works after all.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We have a few configuration items we'd like to point out.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Rotational hand controller power, Direct; BMAGS, Att 1, Rate 2.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: MCC-H are reminding the crew that switches for the rotational hand controller Direct configuration, on panel 1, should both be set to buses MNA & MNB.

When the rotational hand controller is deflected 11 degrees, in this mode, power will be applied to the direct coils of the appropriate RCS engines, and the auto coils will be disable for this axis (or axes).
This permits the crew to directly, manually alter the spacecraft's attitude.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: They are also being reminded to set the BMAG mode for each of the three axes to Att 1, Rate 2.

This BMAG setting allows the gyro assembly 2 to provide rate signals for control and display, plus for the GDC update, and allows gyro assembly 1 to provide attitude error signals for control and display.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: How we are on our residuals?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Midcourse correction burn number 7 scheduled for 7 seconds duration, was actually burned out at 6.6 seconds.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: It's starting to get real cool in here.
We'd like to go Bypass on the suit circuit heat exchanger.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Placing the Suit Circuit Heat Exch switch panel 2 in the Bypass position, prevents the flow of water-glycol through the suit heat exchanger, allowing the suit circuit temperature to increase.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And we can turn it on if it gets warm again.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Could you give me a short count, and I'll cut off my S-band and see whether I can pick you up on VHF?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We'll try a VHF voice check as soon as we get the sites configured and you can go to left antenna.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Set up your VHF and S-band turned down.
We're going to try the VHF check in a minute.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Apollo 10, we're back up on S-band.
We weren't able to read you on VHF.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We heard some kind of transmissions there in the background, but we never gave you a call on VHF.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I asked for a radio check on VHF.
Apparently we're not quite in range yet.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Apollo 10 now 24,469 nautical miles [45,317 km] out from Earth; velocity now 12,794 feet per second [3.900 m/s].
Still monitoring air to ground, live.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: All sites are monitoring VHF downlink.
When you're ready, make a transmission and they'll see if we can pick you up.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: This is Apollo 10 transmitting on VHF on the short count.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Sites apparently aren't reading you yet on VHF.
We recommend that you transmit simultaneous S-band and VHF; and, when the sites can read VHF, why, they'll let us know, and we'll conduct the radio check.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We're all squared away and way ahead on the checklist.
The next thing we're waiting for is just to read out the Command Module RCS temps and service the primary Evap and the logic check coming up.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And there's no significant change in the weather in the landing area.
The altimeter is 29.88, or plus 38 feet, and we have a splashdown computed time of 192:03, and sunrise will be 25 minutes later.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The Command Module has an altimeter, similar to those found on aircraft that rely on the measurement of air pressure to determine altitude.
It is sited just above the EMS panel.

In order for an aircraft altimeter to be accurate, compensation must be made for the sea level air pressure which, at the time, was conventionally stated in terms of inches of mercury (the height of a column of mercury whose weight would balance the weight of the column of air creating the air pressure at that point); in this case 29.88 inches.
However, as John will point out, unlike an aircraft altimeter, there is no provision for setting the sea level pressure on the Apollo version.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hey, Jack, we don't have any place to set the altimeter.
But thanks for the plus 38 feet.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And, 10, we're ready for your RCS temps when you're ready to read them down.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Houston, here's our readings: 5-Charlie is 5.0; 5-Delta is 4.8; 6-Alpha is 5.1; 6-Bravo is 5.1; 6-Charlie is 4.2; 6-Delta is 4.9.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: These readings from the system test meter on panel 101 are checking the CM RCS temperatures.
So long as they all read above 3.9 VDC, no pre-heating will be required.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: These readings translate to:5C -CM RCS system B - pitch engine oxidizer valve 5.0 VDC = 50°F5D -CM RCS system B + yaw engine oxidizer valve 4.8 VDC = 46°F6A -CM RCS system A counter clockwise roll engine oxidizer valve 5.1 VDC = 52°F6B -CM RCS system A - pitch engine oxidizer valve 5.1 VDC = 52°F6C -CM RCS system A - yaw engine oxidizer valve 4.2 VDC = 34°F6D -CM RCS system B clockwise roll engine oxidizer valve 4.9 VDC = 48°F
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, at 45 minutes to EI - to RRT time Could you give us a time hack?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: You want a time hack at 45 minutes to EI.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: And your computer landing time precisely is now 192:03:57.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Houston, we're going back On with the suit circuit heat exchangers.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Placing the Suit Circuit Heat Exch switch on panel 2 in the Bypass position, has had the desired effect, the crew are more comfortable, so Young is returning it to the Flow position to allow the suit circuit heat exchange to cool the suit gases and remove moisture in the circuit.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Late correction on your landing time is 192:02:57.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Buddy, you're going to get there.
It doesn't really matter does it?
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We could put you in a holding pattern for a minute there, maybe.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: As long as our RET times are OK, we're in good shape.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Wish you would relay on to Captain Cruse, the skipper of the Princeton, that at the time there, at 192:03, we expect to be right on top of the aim point and hope this big ship is close by.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We'll pass the word on, and you just holler "Meat Ball" when you see it.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The 'meat ball' is an optical system used on aircraft carriers to indicate to an incoming pilot that they were on the correct glide slope as they approached the carrier's deck.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: To recap that last exchange, predicted touchdown time, in Ground Elapsed Time 192 hours, 2 minutes, 57 seconds.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Our data up here shows no preheat.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: As the temperatures of the CM RCS are all above 28°F, no pre-heat is required to condition them for use.
Therefore as no additional electrical power will be required for the pre-heat, it will not be necessary to bring the limping fuel cell 1 back on line to supplement the two healthy fuel cells.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Mark; 2 hours, 30 minutes to Entry Interface, or sensible atmosphere at 400,000 feet (121,920 metres] above the surface.
Apollo 10 now 22,150 nautical miles [41,022 km] out from Earth.
Velocity beginning to increase - air to ground here.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The glycol Evap water-flow valve will come on in two zero minutes.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'll leave it On for 3; then to Auto.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: 189:19:42Velocity now 13,399 feet per second [4,084 m/s], beginning to build up as they get back closer into Earth - air to ground still live.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We would like to have all heaters and fans Off in the Cryo tanks.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Heaters and fans going off in the Cryo tanks.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Houston, the Evap servicing is complete, and for the record, of course, we had been there before, but I had gone to Manual Increase again on the valve.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: In an attempt to ensure the primary Glycol Evap does not dry out as it did at the beginning of the mission, Cernan, during the its servicing, selected on panel 2, Steam Press Man which permits manual operational of the steam pressure valve actuator, and Incr which is operated momentarily to increase the steam pressure.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And I know I've got the water flow in Auto.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Cernan also has the H2O Flow switch on panel 2 in the Auto position to apply power to the steam press/wetness control unit and allows automatic regulation of the flow of water to the water-glycol evaporator.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're requesting an in-route descent commencing 1200 Out, and we'd also like expeditious handling, CCA down near the Princeton.
We'll be making a vertical descent from about 24,000 (ft, 7,300 metres].
And request No Go-around this pass.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Report crossing 25 miles (40 km) at 6,000 [ft, 1,800 metres].
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Houston approach hasn't changed a bit.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We hope on this one, Houston approach at 6,000 [ft, 1,800 metres] will be within about a half to a quarter of a mile [0.8 to 0.4 km].
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: You can write your deviation up when you're on the ground.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The crew are jovially using aeronautical terminology to request a priority approach to the splashdown point, with no go-around option as they are coming in ready or not, vertically.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: What's our range to Mother Earth?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're reading 19,660 [nautical miles, 36,410 km] at this time.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: You're coming in about 14,100 feet per second [4,300 m/s] right now.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: It's that last 10,000 miles (18,520 km) that's more interesting anyway.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We still have lots of propellant in our primary propellant tanks, but we wanted to know, for the Service Module jettison, if you want us to open the secondary propellants.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The SM RCS will be automatically fire.
The -X SM RCS engines will fire to assist separation and cause the SM to tumble during its atmospheric entry, which will speed up its destruction.
The RCS will fire until propellant depletion, so MCC-H are not concerned with the quantity remaining.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're ready for the logic sequence check now.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We're ready to go with the check.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The ELS logic is coming on.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We'll give you a Go for Pyro Arm.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The Sequential Events Control Subsystem logic circuit is used to provide part of the serial protection of the circuits that command pyrotechnic ordnance devices that initiate the several sequential events, such as CM-SM Sep.
The Seq Event Control System switches, 1 & 2, are located on panel 8.
When placed in the Logic position, either switch energizes the relays that connect the Main Event Sequence Controller logic circuits to battery buses A & B.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We completed the sextant star check and Altair was within about 6 minutes of being right in the middle of the optics.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: The sextant check is performed to give additional confidence that the spacecraft attitude is correct.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Do you want to remain High Gain here until just before Sep?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let's stay in High Gain until Sep and then go to Sep C.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: That's Sep Charlie; is that right, Jack?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let's stay in High Gain until that time.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I think you're lucky there in a Noun 05.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Young is performing a P52 IMU realignment, option 3, realigning it to the current REFSMMAT.

The Noun 05 that Lousma is referring to is the star angle difference displayed on the DSKY as xxx.xx degrees when two stars are sequentially sighted through the sextant, when checking the realignment accuracy.
The computer knows the angle between the two stars, thanks to into internal table of their positions.
It also knows the angle between John's two sightings, and compares them.
a reading of zero means the two angles match and there is no difference.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Wait until you see the next one.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: That's not bad for a young fellow.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: You're just a show off; that's all.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: José can work with those optics.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Who's doing that, you or José?
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: That's José; I'm just narrating here.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Our tracking data now shows you right in the middle of the fairway with a 6.53-degree entry angle.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Right in the middle of the corridor with 6.53.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, we checked Noun 61 and it looks loaded correctly now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Our sextant - our alignment check sextant star on Nunki - the Auto optics put the star within the center of the reticle.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: The Noun 61 that Lousma is referring to is the display of the input splashdown latitude and longitude, as derived from the Entry PAD, along with the appropriate Roll Attitude Code., + 1 for heads up / lift vector down, or in this case, - 1 for heads down / lift vector up.
MCC-H are happy that is data has been entered correctly.
This is the first part of the configuration of Program 61, the entry preparation program, which starts the navigation, checks the IMU alignment.
and provide Entry Monitor System initialization data.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The EMS is tested in readiness for the entry.

The test steps are listed below:EMS FUNC - Offcircuit breaker EMS (2) - closeEMS Mode - STBYEMS FUNC - EMS TEST 1 (wait 5 sec)EMS Mode - Normal (wait 10 sec)Check indicator lights - offRange indicator - 0.0Slew hair line over notchIn self-test patternEMS FUNC - EMS TEST 2 (wait 10 sec).05G light - on (all others out)EMS FUNC - EMS TEST 3.05G light - onRSI Lower light - on (10 sec later)Set Range counter to 58 nm0.0EMS FUNC - EMS TEST 4.05G light - on (all others out)G-V trace within pattern to lower right corner @9GRange indicator counts down to 00.2EMS FUNC - EMS TEST 5.05G light - onRSI upper light - on (10 sec later)Range indicator - 0.0Scribe traces vertical line 9g to 0.220.1ALIGN SCROll TO Entry PATTERN (on 37K ft sec line)EMS FUNC - RNG SETG-V scroll assembly traces vertical line 0.22g to 00.1EMS Mode - STBY
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: These are the five EMS tests:EMS test 1 -Tests the lower trip point of the 0.05 G - threshold comparator and enables slewing of the scroll.EMS test 2 -Tests the high trip point of the 0.05 G - threshold comparator.EMS test 3 -Test the lower trip point of the corridor verification comparator and enables slewing of the Delta-V/Range display for EMS test 4 operations.EMS test 4 -Tests the range-to-go integrator circuits, G servo circuits, G - V plotter and range-to-go circuits.EMS test 5 -Tests the high trip point of corridor verification comparator and enables slewing of the scroll.These five tests are carried out to ensure the EMS is ready and configured for the upcoming entry.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, as I was driving the scroll down to - down to the pattern, the thing stopped scribing.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I say, the EMS stopped scribing as I was driving it down to the test pattern to set it up on the exit range.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Following the EMS tests the scribe head is positioned at the 37K fps (11,270 m/s] mark on the next non-exit scroll pattern in the EMS display window.

The formulation of the emulsion on the scroll film had recently been changed and this may have caused the emulsion to harden to much, so for Apollo 13 onwards the emulsion reverted to the original formulation.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Oh, you mean driving it down to 37 K?
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: You say the needle's not scraping, or it's not driving in this tape?
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: It's not - the tape is driving, but the needle is not leaving any mark.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I believe I'll go ahead and run it on down here anyway.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Get some gs on it; it might work OK.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Have I got a Go to activate the primary Evaps?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, we are going to maneuver to the Sep attitude now.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: The spacecraft is yawed 45° left, out-of-plane, from the pre-entry attitude to the SM Sep attitude.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: John, if you haven't already tried it, you might, on that EMS scroll, try running the tape back a half inch backwards and back and forth between the 37K line, and if that doesn't work, why, you can try the next pattern, and maybe this will break up that emulsion a little bit.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Run the scroll back and forth between where and where?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Make sure you run it backwards first, backwards no more than half an inch, and then forwards no more than the 37K line.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: What I did was I had it on range set, I went back to test 5.
Does that mean that I have to go all the way through the EMS counterclockwise anymore, or if I go to range set, will it still be initialized at 37K.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Say again how far back you went please.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We're getting a lot of noise on the High Gain in this Sep attitude.
How about me going to Sep at this time?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let's go to Sep Charlie by the checklist.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We'll hold off on it, then; but it's awful noisy up here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Manoeuvring the spacecraft to the Sep attitude has compromised the quality of the High Gain Antenna signal.
The crew want to switch to the CM mounted omnidirectional antenna that they will use post Sep.
At this range the high bit rate communications can be maintained via the CM omni antenna.
MCC-H are instructing the crew to use Omni Charlie which is located on the CM external skin approximately behind window 2.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, we're going back to the initial Entry Interface attitude.
This noise is about to drive us wild up here on that High Gain.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: It's OK to go Sep Charlie, now, if you'd rather.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We can go there now, huh?
Because we're a little bit ahead of the checklist in going into this attitude.
We'll go to Sep Charlie at this time.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It's not clear to us exactly what you did with the EMS.
Will you describe it one more time so we can give you an answer?
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I was on Range Set when you said wiggle it back and forth, so I went back to test 5 and brought it back approximately three-eighths of an inch toward 38K, and it started scribing.
So I quit and went back to 37, and I'm now on test 5.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Do I have to go through another scroll pattern or go clean through the EMS test to get it reset at 37K?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We'll get an answer for you in just a minute.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I think the answer is no.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Apollo 10, while we're coming up with that answer, we're ready to uplink a state vector.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We're a little delayed on our uplink.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Mark 1 minute to Entry Interface at which point the Apollo 10 will come back into the, effectively, the Earth's atmosphere at 400,000 feet [121,920 metres].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I have an update to your entry PAD.
There are only five numbers that are different than the last PAD.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Take them one at a time, will you?
And let me confirm them would you?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: When they reach Entry Interface (400,000 feet or 121.92 km) then their flight path will for an angle with the local horizontal at that point of 6.54°.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That's affirmative, and our RET at 0.05g is 00:27.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The point when they reach an aerodynamic deceleration of 0.05g is expected to occur 27 seconds after Entry Interface.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And the next three numbers are in the blackout block.
Begin blackout at 00:17, end at 03:28, and drogues at 08:16, Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I got begin blackout at 00:17, and blackout at 3:28, and drogues at 8:16.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Blackout will begin at 17 seconds and end at 3 minutes, 28 seconds.
The drogues should deploy at 8 minutes and seconds, all after the 400K point.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: To correct statement earlier in reading the clock it's now 57 minutes to Entry Interface, instead of 1 minute.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Are you finished with the uplink?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Apollo 10, we're finished with the uplink.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Shows we got a 22.2-mile vacuum perigee.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Keying Verb 82 Enter starts Routine 30 which can be used to display current orbital parameters.
A flashing Verb 16, Noun 44 displays the following parameters:R1 - Apogee altitude.Not applicable in the re-entry situation.
XXXX.XR2 - Perigee altitude.Displayed in nautical miles.

XXXX.XR3 - TFF.Time of free fall, in this case it will display the maximum value of -59859 as it is not applicable.As Stafford states, these values apply when the spacecraft is travelling in a vacuum.

As the perigee being computed is 22.2 nautical miles [41.1 km), is well within the sensible Earth atmosphere, the deceleration this atmosphere will impose on the spacecraft, will ensure that the spacecraft returns to the Earth's surface.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And on your EMS, you can go directly to Range Set and it'll work.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: And, Houston, we're ready to activate the secondary Evaps.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let's hold off until EI minus 50, about 6½ more minutes, for the secondary Evap.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Distance is now down to 9,136 nautical miles [16,920 km], velocity building up now at 19,128 feet per second [5,830 m/s].
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We'd like to proceed with the VHF check now, and we're configured at the sites.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We're reading you right now VHF and short count follows: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You came in way down in the mud.
You faded out, then came back in.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: You're about three by and down in the mud.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And I've got one more number to change on your entry PAD.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: It's OK to get on the S-band, huh, Jack?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Read you loud and clear now, 10.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: You're still down in the mud.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The next change is on EMS range to go, should read 12061.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The previously provided EMS Range To Go value has now been amended to 1206.1 nautical miles [2,233.7 km].
This value will be set into the EMS.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It sounds like you're transmitting on VHF and S-band, too, Jack.
Could you go back to S-band only?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We have a change to your altimeter.
Your Delta-H will be a plus 57.
The recovery ship's on station, the aircraft areen route.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I'm going to activate the secondary Evap.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: There's no change in the weather in the landing area; 2,500 [feet, 760 metres], scattered, and 10.
Winds are 120 at 10 knots, a 3-foot [0.9 metres] swell, and we're coming up on our 45-minute check momentarily.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We need a Go for Pyro Arm to check the RCS.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Stand by to fire the Pyros; 3, 2, 1.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: As the CM RCS helium pressurization squib valves are detonated, a thud can be heard on the air-to-ground audio.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: They look good to us, too.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Some 43 minutes now from Entry Interface.
Present distance 6,916 nautical miles [12,808 km] out.
It's reported that Rescue Aircraft Numbers 1, 2, and 3, staging out of Samoa are now on station.
Ground Elapsed Time now 191 hours, 6 minutes.
Continuing to monitor air-ground as the crew approaches the separation time at the Entry Interface minus 15 minutes.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Both rings of the Command Module Reaction Control System have been pressurized.
Pyrotechnics are armed, both rings are showing normal pressure at this time.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Youre looking real good there, John.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The Command Module Reaction Control pressure system is activated, and the pressure can be heard "gurgling" through the lines.
An audible noise indicates both rings of the Command Module Reaction Control System were hot fired satisfactorily.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The three rescue aircraft are C130 herkybird turboprop aircraft.
Rescue 1 aircraft commander is Captain Jerald D.
He is with the 76th Air Rescue squadron out of Hickam Air Force Base, Hawaii.
Ott, Milwaukee, Wisconsin, 79th Air Rescue squadron, Andersen Air Force Base, Guam.
Rescue 3 aircraft commander is Captain Anthony A.
Vanagas, of Carnei, New Jersey, 76th Air Rescue squadron, Hickam Air Force Base.
Standing by on air ground live.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Clip courtesy John Stoll, ACR Senior Technician at NASA Johnson.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The bus ties are on the line okay.
Tape recorder is going to rewind.
And the Sun is setting just like you said.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: It's amazing to see an airglow on the horizon again.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: You guys are a little out of your environment now aren't you?
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Didn't see that up there around the Moon.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: It's a good round one; I'll tell you that.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: 191:20:55This is Apollo Control; present altitude, 4,296 nautical miles [7,956 km]; 27 minutes away from Entry Interface.
About 12 minutes away from separating the Service Module from the Command Module; this takes place at 15 minutes prior to 400,000 feet (121,920 metres], or what is called Entry Interface.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Were halfway through the Sep checklist, and were maneuvering to the separation attitude
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Looks like we might have a primary Evap.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Cernan is confirming the primary steam evaporator appears to be operating satisfactorily, following drying out during launch.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 191:27:06This is Apollo Control, 21 minutes from Entry Interface...
transcript.speaker: PAO
